name: Montana Railroad History
description: GeoJSON mapping data for all 18 Montana Railroad History articles. Features include Point markers for key locations and LineString routes for railroad corridors. Each feature links to its source article via the 'article_file' property.

properties.name: Smith Mine (Bear Creek)
properties.article: The Bear Creek Coal Trains
properties.description: Site of the Smith Mine disaster, February 27, 1943. An explosion killed 74 miners. The mine served the Montana, Wyoming and Southern Railroad coal branch.

properties.name: Red Lodge Depot (Montana, Wyoming and Southern RR)
properties.article: The Bear Creek Coal Trains
properties.description: Southern terminus of the coal branch line from Billings. Red Lodge was the supply and service town for the Bear Creek coal mines.

properties.name: Montana, Wyoming and Southern RR — Coal Branch
properties.article: The Bear Creek Coal Trains
properties.description: The coal branch running from Billings south through Red Lodge to the Bear Creek mines. Operated from 1898 until the Smith Mine closed after the 1943 disaster.

properties.name: Custer Creek Train Wreck Site
properties.article: The Custer Creek Train Wreck of 1938
properties.description: Site of the June 19, 1938 wreck of the Milwaukee Road's Olympian passenger train. Flash flooding washed out a bridge over Custer Creek. 47 passengers killed. Near Saugus, Prairie County.

properties.name: Milwaukee Road — Eastern Montana (Miles City to Harlowton corridor)
properties.article: The Custer Creek Train Wreck of 1938
properties.description: The Milwaukee Road's main line across eastern Montana. The Olympian and Columbian passenger trains ran this route. The Custer Creek wreck occurred near Saugus.
properties.railroad: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad

properties.name: Northern Pacific Completion Site, Gold Creek
properties.article: The Golden Spike at Gold Creek
properties.description: Site of the Northern Pacific Railroad completion ceremony, September 8, 1883. Henry Villard drove the final spike in the presence of Ulysses S. Grant and 300 guests. Now a National Historic Landmark.

properties.name: Northern Pacific Railway — Montana Main Line
properties.article: The Golden Spike at Gold Creek
properties.description: The Northern Pacific transcontinental main line across Montana, completed September 8, 1883. Runs from the North Dakota border through Glendive, Miles City, Billings, Livingston, Bozeman, Butte, Missoula, and into Idaho.

properties.name: Butte — BA&P Eastern Terminus
properties.article: The Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
properties.description: Eastern terminus of the Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway at the Butte mine yards. The BA&P hauled copper ore from the mines to the Anaconda smelter 26 miles west. Electrified 1913.

properties.name: Anaconda Copper Smelter
properties.article: The Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
properties.description: The Anaconda Copper smelter, western terminus of the BA&P. Marcus Daly built the smelter in 1884 to process Butte copper ore. The 585-foot Anaconda Stack still stands as a state park.

properties.name: Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
properties.article: The Butte, Anaconda and Pacific Railway
properties.description: The 26-mile BA&P connecting Butte mines to the Anaconda smelter. First major electrified railroad in the US (1913). Operated electric locomotives until 1967. Abandoned 1985.

properties.name: Harlowton — Jawbone Railroad Western Terminus
properties.article: The Jawbone: Richard Harlow's Impossible Railroad
properties.description: Western terminus of the Montana Railroad (the 'Jawbone'), built by Richard Harlow. The line ran from Lombard on the Northern Pacific to Lewistown. Harlow financed it largely on talk — hence the nickname.

properties.name: Lewistown — Jawbone Railroad Eastern Terminus
properties.article: The Jawbone: Richard Harlow's Impossible Railroad
properties.description: Eastern terminus of the Jawbone Railroad at Lewistown, the largest city in central Montana. The line served the agricultural and mining communities of the Judith Basin.

properties.name: Montana Railroad (The Jawbone) — Lombard to Lewistown
properties.article: The Jawbone: Richard Harlow's Impossible Railroad
properties.description: Richard Harlow's 'Jawbone Railroad' running from Lombard (NP junction) through Harlowton to Lewistown. Built 1900-1903 on credit and promises. Absorbed by the Milwaukee Road in 1908.

properties.name: Marias Pass Summit
properties.article: The Discovery of Marias Pass
properties.description: Marias Pass, elevation 5,213 ft — the lowest crossing of the Continental Divide in the northern Rocky Mountains. John Frank Stevens discovered it for the Great Northern Railway on December 11, 1889, walking alone in temperatures of -40°F.

properties.name: John Frank Stevens Monument
properties.article: The Discovery of Marias Pass
properties.description: Monument to John Frank Stevens at Marias Pass, honoring his solo winter discovery of the pass in 1889. A statue of Theodore Roosevelt also stands nearby.

properties.name: Harlowton — Eastern End of Rocky Mountain Electrification
properties.article: The Milwaukee Road Electrification
properties.description: Harlowton was the eastern end of the Milwaukee Road's Rocky Mountain Division electrification (1915). Electric locomotives took over here from steam for the mountain crossing to Avery, Idaho.

properties.name: Milwaukee Road — Rocky Mountain Division (Electrified, 1915-1974)
properties.article: The Milwaukee Road Electrification
properties.description: The Milwaukee Road's electrified Rocky Mountain Division from Harlowton, MT to Avery, ID. 440 miles of 3,000V DC overhead wire. First major mainline electrification in the US. Operated 1915-1974.
properties.railroad: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad

properties.name: Billings — Northern Pacific Land Office
properties.article: Sodbusters and the Railroad Land Promoters
properties.description: Billings was platted by the Northern Pacific in 1882 and named for railroad president Frederick Billings. The NP's land office here sold railroad grant lands to homesteaders lured by promotional literature promising adequate rainfall and fertile soil.

properties.name: Livingston — Northern Pacific Division Point
properties.article: Sodbusters and the Railroad Land Promoters
properties.description: Livingston was the Northern Pacific's division point for the Yellowstone branch and the gateway to Yellowstone National Park. The 1902 depot still stands. Named for NP director Crawford Livingston.

properties.name: Helena — Chinese Railroad Worker Community
properties.article: The Chinese Forerunners of the Northern Pacific
properties.description: Helena's Chinatown was established in part by Chinese workers who graded the Northern Pacific right-of-way through the mountains. An estimated 15,000 Chinese laborers worked on the NP's Montana construction in the 1880s.

properties.name: Missoula — Northern Pacific Mountain Division
properties.article: The Chinese Forerunners of the Northern Pacific
properties.description: Missoula was a key point on the Northern Pacific's mountain division, where Chinese laborers performed much of the grading through the Clark Fork canyon. The Missoula NP depot (1901) still stands.

properties.name: Browning — Blackfeet Reservation (Great Northern Route)
properties.article: The Iron Horse and the Blackfeet Nation
properties.description: Browning is the seat of the Blackfeet Nation. The Great Northern Railway crossed Blackfeet territory under an 1895 agreement that ceded the mineral strip along the Rocky Mountain Front — land that became the eastern portion of Glacier National Park in 1910.

properties.name: Cut Bank — Great Northern / Blackfeet Territory
properties.article: The Iron Horse and the Blackfeet Nation
properties.description: Cut Bank sits on the edge of the Blackfeet Reservation. The Great Northern's Hi-Line route passed through here, bisecting Blackfeet hunting territory and accelerating the end of the buffalo economy.

properties.name: East Glacier Park Station
properties.article: See America First: The Great Northern and Glacier Park
properties.description: East Glacier Park Station, built 1912. Gateway to Glacier Park Lodge, the Great Northern's flagship hotel. Still an active Amtrak Empire Builder stop (seasonal). The station and lodge were the centerpiece of the GN's 'See America First' tourism campaign.

properties.name: Whitefish Station (Empire Builder)
properties.article: See America First: The Great Northern and Glacier Park
properties.description: Whitefish Station, the western gateway to Glacier National Park on the Great Northern/BNSF main line. Active Amtrak Empire Builder stop. The 1927 depot is on the National Register of Historic Places.

properties.name: Great Northern Railway — Glacier Park Corridor
properties.article: See America First: The Great Northern and Glacier Park
properties.description: The Great Northern's route through Glacier National Park's southern edge, from Cut Bank through East Glacier and Marias Pass to Whitefish. The corridor that James J. Hill used to promote 'America's Alps' to tourists.

properties.name: St. Paul Pass Tunnel — East Portal (Montana)
properties.article: The St. Paul Pass Tunnel
properties.description: East portal of the St. Paul Pass (Taft) Tunnel on the Montana-Idaho border. 8,771 feet long, completed 1908. The Milwaukee Road bored through the Bitterroot Range at 4,157 ft elevation. Now the eastern trailhead of the Route of the Hiawatha bike trail.

properties.name: St. Paul Pass Tunnel — West Portal (Idaho)
properties.article: The St. Paul Pass Tunnel
properties.description: West portal of the St. Paul Pass Tunnel near Avery, Idaho. The tunnel was the key engineering achievement of the Milwaukee Road's Pacific Extension. Now the western end of the Route of the Hiawatha trail.

properties.name: Harlowton — Milwaukee Road Abandonment Point
properties.article: The Longest Ghost: Abandoning the Milwaukee Road
properties.description: Harlowton was the eastern end of the Milwaukee Road's Pacific Extension in Montana. When the railroad abandoned its western lines in 1980, the tracks were pulled up and the right-of-way fell silent. The Milwaukee Road depot still stands.

properties.name: Milwaukee Road — Abandoned Montana Right-of-Way (1980)
properties.article: The Longest Ghost: Abandoning the Milwaukee Road
properties.description: The Milwaukee Road's abandoned right-of-way across Montana. Tracks pulled up 1980. Portions now converted to trail use including the Route of the Hiawatha near St. Paul Pass. The rest is a ghost corridor visible on satellite imagery.
properties.railroad: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad

properties.name: Fort Benton — Missouri River Levee
properties.article: The End of the Steamboat Era at Fort Benton
properties.description: Fort Benton's levee on the Missouri River — the farthest inland port in North America. Steamboats traveled 2,600 miles from St. Louis to reach here. The arrival of the Great Northern Railway in 1887 ended the steamboat era. The levee and historic district are preserved.

properties.name: Fort Benton — Great Northern Depot
properties.article: The End of the Steamboat Era at Fort Benton
properties.description: The Great Northern Railway reached Fort Benton in 1887, ending the steamboat trade that had sustained the town since 1846. The depot replaced the levee as the center of commerce.

properties.name: Whitefish — Empire Builder Station
properties.article: Forty-Five Hours Through the Mountains: The Empire Builder
properties.description: Whitefish is the primary Montana stop for Amtrak's Empire Builder, the direct descendant of the Great Northern's flagship train inaugurated June 10, 1929. The train runs Chicago to Seattle/Portland, 2,206 miles.

properties.name: Empire Builder Route — Montana (Great Northern / BNSF Hi-Line)
properties.article: Forty-Five Hours Through the Mountains: The Empire Builder
properties.description: The Empire Builder's route across Montana on the former Great Northern Hi-Line, from Williston ND through Havre, Shelby, Cut Bank, East Glacier, Marias Pass, and Whitefish. Active Amtrak service daily.

properties.name: Butte — Miners Union Hall Site (1914 Riot)
properties.article: The Richest Hill on Earth: Butte, 1914
properties.description: Site of the Butte Miners Union Hall, dynamited June 13, 1914. The WFM Local No. 1 had stood for 30 years. Its destruction ended union recognition at the Anaconda mines and began six years of National Guard occupation. The site is now a parking lot.

properties.name: Granite Mountain / Speculator Mine Memorial
properties.article: The Richest Hill on Earth: Butte, 1914
properties.description: Site of the Granite Mountain / Speculator Mine fire, June 8, 1917. 168 miners killed — the worst hard rock mining disaster in US history. The memorial stands on the hill above Uptown Butte.

properties.name: Billings — NP-Platted Town, 1882
properties.article: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
properties.description: Billings was platted by the Northern Pacific in 1882 and named for railroad president Frederick Billings. It grew to become Montana's largest city. The original NP townsite grid is still visible in the downtown street layout.

properties.name: Miles City — Northern Pacific Division Point
properties.article: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
properties.description: Miles City grew around the Northern Pacific's arrival in 1881. Named for General Nelson Miles, it became the cattle-shipping capital of eastern Montana and a key NP division point.

properties.name: Glendive — Northern Pacific Division Point
properties.article: A Town Every Ten Miles: The Northern Pacific and the Making of Montana
properties.description: Glendive was established by the Northern Pacific in 1881 as a division point near the Montana-North Dakota border. The NP depot was the economic center of Dawson County for decades.

properties.name: Butte — Copper Kings Mine District
properties.article: The War of the Copper Kings
properties.description: The Butte mine district where Marcus Daly, William A. Clark, and F. Augustus Heinze fought for control of the world's richest copper deposits from the 1880s through 1906. The Anaconda Company emerged as the monopoly. The Berkeley Pit is the legacy.

properties.name: Anaconda — Daly's Smelter and Company Town
properties.article: The War of the Copper Kings
properties.description: Anaconda was Marcus Daly's company town, built around his copper smelter in 1884. The 585-foot Anaconda Stack is the tallest surviving masonry structure in the world. The BA&P Railroad connected it to Butte.

properties.name: Great Falls — Boston and Montana Smelter
properties.article: The War of the Copper Kings
properties.description: Great Falls was the site of the Boston and Montana Company's smelter, the second-largest copper operation in Montana after Daly's Anaconda. The Great Northern Railway served it. The company was eventually absorbed by Anaconda.
